Notice how the boundaries of the Mughal Empire were reshaped... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 8 EIGHTEENTH-CENTURY, POLITICAL FORMATIONS, 95EIGHTEENTH-CENTURY, 97EIGHTEENTH-CENTURY. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Devotional Paths to the Divine is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 94 our pasts – ii 8 EIGHTEENTH-CENTURY POLITICAL FORMATIONS If you look at Maps 1 and 2 closely, you will see something significant happening in the subcontinent during the first half of the eighteenth century.

The text explains that notice how the boundaries of the Mughal Empire were reshaped by the emergence of a number of independent Map 1 State formations in the eighteenth century.

The text explains that 95EIGHTEENTH-CENTURY POLITICAL FORMATIONS kingdoms.

The text explains that by 1765, notice how another power, the British, had successfully grabbed major chunks of territory in eastern India.

The text explains that what these maps tell us is that political conditions in eighteenth-century India changed quite dramatically and within a relatively short span of time.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will read about the e m e r g e n c e o f n e w political groups in the subcontinent during the first half of the eighteenth century – roughly from 1707, when Aurangzeb died, till the third battle of Panipat in 1761.

The text explains that the Crisis of the Empire and the Later Mughals In Chapter 4, you saw how the Mughal Empire reached the height of its success and started facing a variety of crises towards the closing years of the seventeenth century.

The text explains that emperor Aurangzeb had depleted the military and financial resources of his empire by fighting a long war in the Deccan.

The text explains that under his successors, the efficiency of the imperial administration broke down.

The text explains that it became increasingly difficult for the later Mughal emperors to keep a check on their powerful mansabdars.

The text explains that nobles appointed as governors (subadars) often controlled the offices ?

The text explains that which group of people challenged Mughal authority for the longest time in Aurangzeb’s reign?

The text explains that map 2 British territories in the mid-eighteenth century.

The text explains that 96 our pasts – ii of revenue and military administration (diwani and faujdari) as well.

The text explains that this gave them extraordinary political, economic and military powers over vast regions of the Mughal Empire.

The text explains that as the governors consolidated their control over the provinces, the periodic remission of revenue to the capital declined.

The text explains that these revolts were sometimes caused by the pressures of mounting taxes.

The text explains that but these groups were now able to seize the economic resources of the region to consolidate their positions.

The text explains that already under severe pressure from all sides, the empire was further weakened by competition amongst different groups of nobles.

The text explains that maratha campaigns into Rajasthan from the 1740s put severe pressure on these principalities and checked their further expansion.

The text explains that with extensive fortifications, t h e s e m a j e s t i c s t r u c t u re s housed urban centres, palaces, temples, trading centres, water harvesting structures and other buildings.

The text explains that 100 our pasts – ii Seizing Independence The Sikhs The organisation of the Sikhs into a political community during the seventeenth century (see Chapter 6) helped in regional state-building in the Punjab.

The text explains that do you recall reading about it in Chapter 6? ?

The text explains that a system called rakhi was introduced, offering protection to cultivators on the payment of a tax of 20 per cent of the produce.

The text explains that by introducing an efficient administrative system supported by a revenue collection method based on chauth and sardeshmukhi, he laid the foundations of a strong Maratha state.

The text explains that he possessed the right to levy chauth and sardeshmukhi in the entire region.

The text explains that expansion brought enormous resources, but it came at a price.

The text explains that alongside endless military campaigns, the Marathas developed an effective administrative system as well.

The text explains that this allowed Maratha chiefs (sardars) like Sindhia of Gwalior, Gaekwad of Baroda and Bhonsle of Nagpur the resources to raise powerful armies.

The text explains that chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj (1630- 1680) Chhatrapati Sambhaji (1681- 1689) Chhatrapati Rajaram (1689- 1700) Maharani Tarabai (1700-1761) Shahu Maharaj (Son of Sambhaji) (1682-1749) Source: R.

The text explains that did any of the kingdoms mentioned in this chapter develop in your state?

The text explains that state whether true or false: (a) Nadir Shah invaded Bengal.

The text explains that (b) Sawai Raja Jai Singh was the ruler of Indore.

The text explains that (c) Guru Gobind Singh was the tenth Guru of the Sikhs.

The text explains that (d) Poona became the capital of the Marathas in the eighteenth century.

The text explains that how were the Sikhs organised in the eighteenth century?

The text explains that why did the Marathas want to expand beyond the Deccan?

The text explains that do you think merchants and bankers today have the kind of influence they had in the eighteenth century?

The text explains that if so, in what ways do you think life in the state would have been different in the eighteenth century from what it is in the twenty- first century?

The text explains that collect popular tales about-rulers from any one of the following groups of people: the Rajputs, Jats, Sikhs or Marathas.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Our Pasts II, Chapter 8: Devotional Paths to the Divine

prelims_traps: Do not treat 8 EIGHTEENTH-CENTURY and POLITICAL FORMATIONS as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Devotional Paths to the Divine; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.

Use 8 EIGHTEENTH-CENTURY and POLITICAL FORMATIONS to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
